title: Fitting a fireplace tiled hearth
subtitle: Lay heat-resistant tiles on a concrete hearth pad in front of a fireplace
excerpt: How to tile a fireplace hearth using heat-resistant ceramic or natural stone tiles and a suitable adhesive, covering the setting out, cutting tiles around the front of the fire surround, and grouting and sealing the finished surface.
sourceNotes: British Ceramic Tile Council guidance on hearth tiling. Building Regulations Approved Document J (combustion appliances). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The decorative upper hearth surface in front of a fireplace, typically tiled or made of stone, sitting on top of the structural constructional hearth below. Building Regulations require the superimposed hearth to extend at least 300 mm in front of the fireplace opening and at least 150 mm to each side.

A cement-based tile adhesive rated for use in temperatures up to 100 to 120 degrees Celsius. Required for tiles within 500 mm of an open fire or stove. Standard wall-tile adhesive softens and loses bond strength at elevated temperatures.
Grout formulated with fine aggregate for filling joints wider than 3 mm. Natural stone tiles on hearths typically require 5 to 8 mm joints, which need sanded grout to resist shrinkage cracking.

content.text: The
superimposed hearth

is the visible tiled surface in front of the firebox opening. Building Regulations set its minimum size: 300 mm forward of the fireplace opening and 150 mm to either side. The tiles must be fixed with a
heat-resistant tile adhesive
where they are within 500 mm of the firebox. This is a one-afternoon job on a prepared concrete pad; the curing period before the fireplace can be used is 24 to 48 hours.
What you will need: materials

Heat-resistant ceramic or porcelain hearth tiles (slate, quarry tile, and encaustic tile are all suitable; standard wall tiles are not). Heat-resistant flexible tile adhesive, grey or white depending on the tile colour. Tile spacers at the joint width you prefer (3 mm for a close joint, 5 to 8 mm for a traditional joint with natural stone or quarry tile). Sanded tile grout in a colour matched to the tiles. Tile primer if the concrete pad is very absorbent or dusty. Grout sealer for natural stone and unglazed quarry tiles.
Step 1, prepare the concrete pad

Sweep the concrete pad clear of ash and debris. Use the 50 mm scraper to lift any old adhesive or grout that is standing proud of the surface. Check the pad for level with the spirit level: it must be within 3 mm over the whole area. A slight slope toward the room is acceptable; a step or lip at the edge of the surround will make the tile joint at that point impossible to keep even. Fill any hollows or cracks with a skim of bonding compound and allow to dry before proceeding. If the pad is dusty or powdery, paint it with a PVA-and-water primer at a 1:4 dilution before applying adhesive.
Step 2, set out the tile positions

Dry-lay the tiles on the pad without adhesive to check the layout. The front edge of the hearth is the most visible line: start here and work toward the fireplace. If the tiles leave a narrow cut at the back edge (less than 30 mm), shift the layout forward by half a tile width to keep even-sized cuts at both front and back. Mark the tile positions with a pencil on the pad before lifting the dry layout. Keep the layout symmetrical about the centre line of the fireplace opening: any asymmetry at the fire surround stands out.
Step 3, apply adhesive and lay the tiles

Mix the heat-resistant adhesive to a firm but workable consistency. Spread it on the pad using the flat edge of the filling knife, then comb it in one direction with a notched edge (improvise by cutting notches in a piece of hardboard if you do not have a notched trowel). The combed ridges must not exceed 6 mm for a standard tile. Press each tile firmly into the bed, twisting it slightly to collapse the ridges and achieve full contact. Insert spacers between tiles immediately. Check the surface for level across adjacent tiles with the spirit level before the adhesive sets.

Work from the room outward toward the fireplace: this avoids kneeling on laid tiles and disturbing them before they set. Cut the tiles at the back and sides last; use a tile scorer and breaker for straight cuts on glazed ceramic, a diamond wet saw for natural stone. Allow the adhesive to cure for 24 hours before grouting.
Step 4, grout and seal
Remove the tile spacers and clean any adhesive from the joints. Mix the
sanded grout

to a stiff paste and press it into the joints with the filling knife, working at a diagonal to the joint lines. Clean the tile faces with a damp sponge before the grout starts to dry. Allow 24 hours before the fireplace is used. For unglazed quarry tiles, encaustic tiles, and natural stone, apply a proprietary grout sealer over the whole surface (tiles and joints) once the grout has cured, following the label timings.
Variations

Victorian and Edwardian fireplaces used 150 x 150 mm encaustic or quarry tiles at the front, with narrow border tiles along the side returns. Reproduction encaustic tiles are available from specialist suppliers. For a modern fireplace with a wide opening, large-format porcelain tiles (600 x 600 mm) give a clean look. They need a perfectly level pad and full back-buttering on every tile.
Care

Clean the hearth with a damp cloth and a pH-neutral cleaner. Avoid acid-based cleaners on natural stone and unglazed tiles. Reseal unglazed and natural stone tiles annually with a penetrating sealer if they are heavily used. Check the grout joints at the base of the fire surround every few years: the junction between the fixed surround and the floor is the most vulnerable joint and benefits from a flexible sealant rather than rigid grout at that single line.

attrs.symptom: Tile lifts or sounds hollow after the fire has been used
attrs.cause: Standard adhesive used instead of heat-resistant; bond softened under temperature.
attrs.fix: Lift the affected tile, clean both surfaces thoroughly, and relay with heat-resistant adhesive. If multiple tiles have lifted, all need relaying; the damaged adhesive across the whole pad has likely weakened.

attrs.symptom: Grout cracks at the joint between the tiles and the fire surround
attrs.cause: Movement between the surround and the hearth, or the joint was grouted rigid when it should have been left flexible.
attrs.fix: Rake out the cracked joint, clean, and fill with a flexible heat-resistant sealant rather than grout. The line where a fixed surround meets the hearth always moves slightly with thermal cycling.

attrs.symptom: Tiles at the back of the hearth are noticeably narrower than planned
attrs.cause: The fireplace opening is not square, or the front edge tile was not set parallel to the room.
attrs.fix: Prevention: always set out from the front edge and check it is parallel to the fire surround before laying. If the tiles are already down, the narrow cut at the back is manageable if it is at least 30 mm wide; anything narrower will eventually lift.
